title: Overbrook Management Corp Acquires New Holdings in AbbVie Inc. $ABBV - MarketBeat
content:
Overbrook Management Corp bought a new position in shares of  AbbVie Inc. (NYSE:ABBV - Free Report) in the fourth quarter, according to its most recent Form 13F filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission. The institutional investor  bought 42,059 shares of the company's stock, valued at approximately $9,610,000. AbbVie makes up   1.7% of Overbrook Management Corp's investment portfolio, making the stock its 21st biggest holding. Other institutional investors have also recently bought and sold shares of the company. Chelsea Counsel Co. bought a new stake in shares of  AbbVie during the third quarter worth $26,000.  Texas Capital Bancshares Inc TX bought a new stake in  AbbVie in the 3rd quarter valued at $31,000.  Evolution Wealth Management Inc. bought a new stake in  AbbVie in the 2nd quarter valued at $26,000.  Caitlin John LLC acquired a new position in  AbbVie in the 3rd quarter worth $33,000.  Finally, Redmont Wealth Advisors LLC bought a new position in  AbbVie during the 3rd quarter worth about $41,000. Institutional investors own  70.23% of the company's stock. In other AbbVie news, SVP David Ryan Purdue sold 5,230 shares of the stock in a transaction on Wednesday, March 4th. The shares were sold at an average price of $233.56, for a total value of $1,221,518.80. Following the completion of the sale, the senior vice president owned 2,654 shares in the company, valued at $619,868.24. This trade represents a 66.34% decrease in their ownership of the stock. The sale was disclosed in a legal filing with the SEC, which is available through this link. Also, EVP Perry C. Siatis sold 22,381 shares of AbbVie stock in a transaction dated Wednesday, February 25th. The shares were sold at an average price of $230.00, for a total transaction of $5,147,630.00. Following the completion of the transaction, the executive vice president directly owned 38,137 shares of the company's stock, valued at approximately $8,771,510. This trade represents a 36.98% decrease in their position. Additional details regarding this sale are available in the official SEC disclosure. 0.08% of the stock is currently owned by company insiders. Several research firms have issued reports on ABBV. Barclays started coverage on AbbVie in a research note on Thursday, February 19th. They issued an "overweight" rating and a $275.00 price target on the stock. Evercore reduced their price objective on AbbVie from $232.00 to $228.00 and set an "outperform" rating for the company in a research report on Wednesday, February 4th. Piper Sandler lifted their target price on AbbVie from $289.00 to $299.00 and gave the company an "overweight" rating in a research report on Wednesday, February 18th. Weiss Ratings  restated a "hold (c)" rating on shares of AbbVie in a research note on Wednesday, January 21st. Finally, William Blair  reaffirmed an "outperform" rating on shares of AbbVie in a research report on Monday, March 9th. Two equities research analysts have rated the stock with a Strong Buy rating, fourteen have assigned  a Buy rating and nine have given a Hold rating to the company. According to MarketBeat, the stock currently has a consensus rating of "Moderate Buy" and a consensus price target of $253.15. NYSE:ABBV opened at $204.89 on Tuesday. AbbVie Inc. has a 52-week low of $164.39 and a 52-week high of $244.81. The company has a market cap of $362.29 billion, a P/E ratio of 86.82, a P/E/G ratio of 0.71 and a beta of 0.34. The stock's 50-day simple moving average is $222.79 and its 200 day simple moving average is $224.80. AbbVie (NYSE:ABBV - Get Free Report) last issued its quarterly earnings results on Wednesday, February 4th. The company reported $2.71 EPS for the quarter, beating the consensus estimate of $2.65 by $0.06. The firm had revenue of $16.62 billion during the quarter, compared to analysts' expectations of $16.39 billion. AbbVie had a negative return on equity of 1,579.75% and a net margin of 6.91%.The business's quarterly revenue was up 10.0% compared to the same quarter last year. During the same period in the prior year, the firm posted $2.16 earnings per share. AbbVie has set its FY 2026 guidance at 14.370-14.570 EPS and its Q1 2026 guidance at 2.970-3.010 EPS.  As a group, equities analysts predict that  AbbVie Inc. will post 12.31 earnings per share for the current fiscal year. The company also recently announced a quarterly dividend, which will be paid on Friday, May 15th. Investors of record on Wednesday, April 15th will be paid a $1.73 dividend. The ex-dividend date of this dividend is Wednesday, April 15th. This represents a $6.92 annualized dividend and a dividend yield of 3.4%. AbbVie's dividend payout ratio (DPR) is currently 293.22%. AbbVie is a global, research-driven biopharmaceutical company that was created as a spin-off from Abbott Laboratories in 2013 and is headquartered in North Chicago, Illinois. The company focuses on discovering, developing and commercializing therapies for complex and often chronic medical conditions. Its operations span research and development, manufacturing, regulatory affairs and commercialization, with an emphasis on bringing specialty medicines to market across multiple therapeutic areas. AbbVie's product portfolio and pipeline cover several major therapeutic categories, including immunology, oncology, neuroscience, virology and women's health. AbbVie (NYSE:ABBV) reported positive topline Phase 1 results for ABBV-295, a long-acting amylin analog for obesity treatment. The trial showed meaningful, dose-dependent reductions in body weight, signaling potential clinical relevance in obesity care. ABBV-295 uses a non-incretin mechanism of action, setting it apart from widely discussed GLP-1 and GIP agonists. The readout marks AbbVie’s entry into the competitive anti-obesity drug market and supports its aim to diversify beyond immunology. AbbVie, best known for its immunology and oncology franchise, is now stepping into obesity therapeutics with ABBV-295. The Phase 1 data arrives at a time when obesity drugs are a key focus for healthcare investors and large pharmaceutical groups, with attention on differentiated mechanisms that sit alongside GLP-1 and GIP drugs. For you as an investor, ABBV-295 adds another angle to the NYSE:ABBV pipeline story, centered on a non-incretin approach to weight management. As future trial stages are reported, the consistency, safety profile, and competitive positioning of ABBV-295 within an evolving obesity market are likely to be key factors to watch. For AbbVie, the ABBV-295 Phase 1 data gives investors a first look at how its obesity ambitions might sit alongside the core immunology and neuroscience story. The trial showed roughly 8% to 10% body weight reductions over 12 to 13 weeks at higher doses in adults with a mean BMI below 30 kg/m², compared with almost flat weight change in placebo. That is an early signal in a small, controlled setting, but it points to a non-incretin, amylin based approach that is different to GLP-1 focused peers such as Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk. If future trials keep backing up this profile, ABBV-295 could add another therapeutic pillar next to drugs like Skyrizi and Rinvoq, which many investors already watch closely. On the flip side, gastrointestinal side effects, mostly mild in this study, and the need to expand into higher BMI, more representative obesity populations leave plenty of work ahead before commercial questions can be addressed. This early obesity data sits alongside the existing narrative that AbbVie is building out new therapy areas, including obesity programs from deals such as the Gubra amylin collaboration, to complement immunology and neuroscience. If ABBV-295 or related obesity assets require higher investment or run into safety or efficacy setbacks, that could challenge the idea that pipeline breadth will comfortably offset risks tied to concentrated exposure in a few major drugs. The current narrative focuses heavily on immunology and neuroscience, so the potential role of a differentiated, non-incretin obesity franchise like ABBV-295 may not yet be fully reflected in how AbbVie’s long term story is framed. From here, it is worth watching how quickly AbbVie moves ABBV-295 into larger, more diverse obesity trials, whether future readouts compare favorably on weight loss and tolerability, and how management describes the role of amylin based therapies next to existing immunology and neuroscience assets. Investors can also track updates on other obesity efforts tied to earlier deals, such as Gubra, to see if AbbVie is building a more complete obesity portfolio or focusing on a narrower set of candidates. title: Gubra-AbbVie obesity drug nears 10% weight loss - Longevity.Technology
content: Various biotech giants have produced and released obesity treatments that have continued to intensify over the past few years, with blockbuster drugs transforming how doctors and investors think about metabolic health. Now a new contender is emerging from a partnership between Danish biotech Gubra and pharmaceutical giant AbbVie. Early clinical results released this week suggest their experimental therapy, ABBV-295, could produce meaningful weight loss in a relatively short time frame [1]. In a Phase 1 multiple ascending dose trial, participants receiving the drug lost between 7.75% and 9.79% of their body weight after about three months of treatment, depending on the dose. By contrast, those receiving a placebo saw almost no change. For an early-stage study, it is striking. Equally important, researchers reported that the drug was generally well-tolerated, with no serious adverse events recorded. “We are very encouraged by these clinical results. Obesity continues to be a growing global health challenge, with a well-recognized need for novel treatment options,” said Markus Rohrwild, CEO of Gubra. “These data demonstrate the potential of ABBV-295 to deliver meaningful body weight reduction with a favorable tolerability profile, supporting its differentiated positioning within the evolving obesity treatment landscape.” To understand why ABBV-295 is drawing attention, it helps to look at how it works. Most of the headline-making obesity drugs today, such as GLP-1 therapies, work by mimicking hormones that regulate appetite and blood sugar. These drugs have proven highly effective, but they all rely on variations of the same biological pathway. ABBV-295 takes a different approach. The therapy is designed to mimic amylin, a naturally occurring hormone released alongside insulin. Amylin acts as one of the body’s internal “stop eating” signals, telling the brain that the stomach is full while also slowing the rate at which food leaves the stomach. In simple terms, it helps the body recognize satiety earlier. The body’s appetite system can be viewed as a dashboard of signals telling you when to start and stop eating. GLP-1 drugs act on one set of controls; amylin-based drugs act on another. Researchers believe targeting multiple signals could eventually lead to more balanced and potentially more sustainable weight management. That possibility is one reason scientists are exploring new mechanisms beyond the current wave of incretin-based treatments. The Phase 1 study evaluated ABBV-295 in adults with an average body mass index below 30, testing several dose levels ranging from 2 mg to 14 mg. Participants received the treatment through injections under the skin. Researchers also explored different dosing schedules. Some participants received weekly injections, while others transitioned to less frequent dosing – every other week or even monthly – later in the trial. Across these variations, a consistent pattern emerged: higher doses led to greater weight loss. After 12 weeks of weekly dosing, participants lost an average of 9.79% of their body weight. Those on placebo experienced only around 0.25% change, essentially a statistical standstill. Side effects were generally manageable. The most commonly reported issues were mild gastrointestinal symptoms, mostly occurring during the first six weeks of treatment. According to Dr Primal Kaur, a senior vice president at AbbVie, obesity is a multifaceted, chronic condition that significantly impacts patients and healthcare infrastructure. She noted that there is an ongoing need for treatments that are both effective and manageable enough for patients to adhere to over the long term. Moreover, Kaur expressed optimism regarding the preliminary data for ABBV-295, stating that the results showed significant weight reduction and a favorable safety profile. Longevity.Technology: At first glance, obesity drugs like ABBV-295 might appear to belong purely to the realm of metabolic medicine. But increasingly, they are becoming central to discussions about longevity. Excess body weight is strongly linked to many of the diseases that shorten healthy lifespan. Addressing those metabolic risk factors earlier in life can significantly alter long-term health outcomes. ABBV-295 represents more than cosmetic weight loss. They are an innovation in preventive medicine, where treating metabolic dysfunction today may help delay the onset of chronic disease decades later. The rapid growth of the obesity therapeutics market was once viewed as a niche category, but it is now a cornerstone of future healthcare. And the next phase of development may not revolve around a single miracle drug. Instead, researchers increasingly envision combination therapies that pair different hormonal pathways (such as GLP-1 and amylin) to amplify benefits while minimizing side effects. The program itself highlights the scale of opportunity investors see in the space. In March 2025, Gubra and AbbVie entered a licensing agreement that gives AbbVie global rights to develop and commercialize ABBV-295. The deal could deliver up to $1.875 billion to Gubra in milestone payments tied to development progress and commercial performance, as well as royalties on global sales. Such deals have become increasingly common as large pharmaceutical companies seek a foothold in what analysts expect to become one of the most lucrative drug markets of the coming decade. ABBV-295 is still an investigational therapy, and much larger clinical trials will be needed before its true potential becomes clear. But early signals like these offer a glimpse into where metabolic medicine may be heading. If the first generation of obesity drugs helped prove that meaningful weight loss through medication is possible, the next generation may focus on refining the biology behind appetite, metabolism and energy balance.
